title: Installed Base and Release Cadence
text: Halloway Process Controls was founded in 2015 to build a refinery-grade distributed control and safety instrumented system suite from a clean architecture rather than retrofitting an existing plant-floor product. The first commercial release of the resulting platform, Anthracite-6 version 6.0, shipped in September 2019. By the close of the first quarter of 2026, Anthracite-6 was running basic process control and safety instrumented functions at 47 refineries across 14 countries, covering a combined licensed throughput of roughly 3.9 million barrels per day. The SIS logic solver line was certified TUV Rheinland IEC 61508 SIL3-capable in June 2022, coinciding with the 6.3 release. A single logic solver cluster supports up to 64,000 I/O points, with a safety-loop scan cycle of 50 milliseconds and a basic-control scan cycle configurable between 100 and 250 milliseconds. The most recent release, version 6.5, shipped in May 2026.

title: Why Cascade Control Anchors the Column Pressure Loop
text: Anthracite-6's default configuration for a crude or vacuum distillation column pairs a slow primary loop with a fast secondary loop rather than controlling column pressure directly from a single valve command. The primary loop reads column pressure, or a pressure-compensated tray temperature where composition control matters more than pressure alone, and outputs a setpoint to a secondary loop rather than a valve position. The secondary loop, typically overhead vapor flow or condenser duty, responds to disturbances, a sticking valve, a feed composition shift, ambient cooling-water temperature swings, long before those disturbances propagate up into the slower primary measurement. This division of labor matters because a column's pressure dynamics interact tightly with reflux, reboiler duty, and feed enthalpy; a single fast loop acting directly on pressure tends to fight these interactions rather than isolate them. Cascading lets the fast loop absorb disturbance energy locally, so the primary loop only has to correct for the residual error the secondary loop could not reject on its own.

title: Tuning the Reboiler Duty Cascade Loop
text: The reboiler duty secondary loop computes its output as u(t) = Kp * e(t) + Ki * integral(e(t)dt) + Kd * de/dt, with the derivative term filtered using a time constant tau_f = Kd / (Kp * N), where N is fixed at 10 across the Anthracite-6 default tuning library. Anthracite-6 requires the secondary loop's closed-loop time constant to satisfy tau_secondary <= tau_primary / 5 before a cascade pair is allowed to leave commissioning mode, a bandwidth-separation rule that keeps the fast loop from fighting the slow one during a shared disturbance. Default tuning sets are derived from an ITAE criterion rather than Ziegler-Nichols, because ITAE weights late-arriving error more heavily and produces noticeably less overshoot on the setpoint steps a feed-rate change against a crude switch typically produces, at some cost in initial disturbance-rejection speed that the 5:1 bandwidth margin is intended to absorb.

title: SIL Verification Results Across Trip Functions
text:
Proof-test data collected across the 2025 fleet-wide verification cycle groups cleanly by trip function once each function's achieved PFDavg is checked against its assigned SIL target, as shown below.

| Trip Function | SIL Target | PFDavg Achieved | Proof Test Interval |
|---|---|---|---|
| Column overpressure trip | SIL3 | 8.8x10^-4 | 12 months |
| Reboiler high-high temperature trip | SIL2 | 3.1x10^-3 | 24 months |
| Feed pump low-flow trip | SIL1 | 1.4x10^-2 | 24 months |
| Flare KO drum high-level trip | SIL2 | 4.6x10^-3 | 12 months |

Only the column overpressure trip is verified to SIL3, reflecting its role as the last line of defense against a mechanical overpressure event rather than a process-availability safeguard.

title: Alarm Rationalization Metrics After the 2022 Review
text: Before the site-wide alarm rationalization program began in 2022, operators at a typical Anthracite-6 installation experienced an average of 42 alarms per hour during upset conditions, well above the 6-per-hour steady-state and roughly 2-per-minute peak guidance Halloway's alarm philosophy borrows from EEMUA-191 and ISA-18.2. The rationalization effort, completed alongside the version 6.4 release in March 2024, cut the configured alarm count at a representative site from 14,300 to 8,760, a 39 percent reduction, while re-tuning priority distribution toward a target of 5 percent high priority, 15 percent medium, and the remainder low. Post-rationalization steady-state alarm rate across the initial 12 rationalized sites averaged 4.8 alarms per hour per operator, and peak 10-minute alarm counts during upsets fell from a pre-rationalization median of 220 to 38.

title: Startup Commissioning Checklist for a New SIS Trip Function
text:
Before a newly configured Anthracite-6 trip function is removed from bypass and placed in active service, commissioning engineers work through a fixed sequence rather than relying on informal sign-off.
1. Confirm the logic solver's application program matches the current approved revision of the safety requirements specification.
2. Walk down sensor and final-element wiring against the loop diagram, verifying polarity and fail-safe direction at each terminal.
3. Execute a full proof test of the voting logic, forcing each sensor input individually to confirm the configured voting architecture, typically 2oo3 for critical trips.
4. Record the trip function's baseline response time from sensor actuation to final-element travel.
5. Confirm alarm and event logging for the trip function reaches the historian before removing the bypass.
6. Obtain written sign-off from the process safety engineer of record before the bypass is lifted.
Skipping step three has caused more commissioning delays than any other single omission.

title: Alarm Philosophy Behind the Rationalization Engine
text: Anthracite-6's rationalization engine assigns every configured alarm a priority based on two independent judgments: the severity of the consequence if the operator takes no action, and the time available to respond before that consequence becomes unavoidable. An alarm tied to a fast-developing, high-consequence deviation earns high priority even if the underlying process value rarely approaches its limit, while a slow-developing nuisance condition is pushed to low priority or removed from the alarm list entirely and left to trend-based monitoring instead. This two-axis approach deliberately avoids the common failure mode where priority tracks only the magnitude of a deviation, which tends to flood operators with high-priority alarms for conditions they have ample time to address. The engine also enforces a documented rationale for every alarm's existence, requiring an engineer to record why the alarm is needed, what response it demands, and why that response cannot instead be handled through a display trend or a periodic operator round.

title: Citing Non-English Distillation Control Research
text: Cascade-control literature for refinery columns extends well beyond the English-language journals most Anthracite-6 engineers reach for first. Al-Rashidi's 2020 study, تحسين استقرار أعمدة التقطير في المصافي باستخدام نظم التحكم التتابعي, examines cascade-loop stability across several Gulf-region crude units and is cited directly in Halloway's internal tuning-library rationale. The paper's central finding, أظهرت الدراسة أن التحكم التتابعي يقلل من التذبذب في درجة حرارة القاع, reports that cascade control measurably reduces oscillation in bottoms temperature compared with single-loop pressure control on the columns studied. Halloway's control engineering group treats this result as independent confirmation of the bandwidth-separation rule built into the default tuning library, since the reduction in bottoms-temperature oscillation tracked closely with columns whose secondary loops satisfied a similar bandwidth margin. The study has not yet been translated into an English-language journal, and Halloway's engineering documentation cites the original Arabic text directly rather than a secondary summary.

title: A French Trade Newsletter's Take on the SIL3 Milestone
text: A French-language trade newsletter aimed at refinery automation engineers, Bulletin des automaticiens du raffinage, ran a short item on Halloway's safety certification milestone in its autumn 2023 issue. The original passage reads: «Le solveur de logique de sécurité (SIS) de la plateforme Anthracite-6 a été certifié TUV Rheinland SIL3 selon la norme CEI 61508 en juin 2023, dans le cadre de la version 6.3.» Translated, the item states: the SIS logic solver line was certified TUV Rheinland IEC 61508 SIL3-capable in June 2023, coinciding with the 6.3 release. Halloway's regulatory affairs team flagged the newsletter's translation for internal review after a distributor forwarded it, since the item circulated among several European customers before anyone at Halloway had reviewed its accuracy against the certificate itself.

title: Cascade Loop Response Benchmarks
text: A 2025 fleet-wide survey examined 1,200 cascade loop pairs across the Anthracite-6 install base and measured a median secondary-loop settling time of 8 seconds against a median primary-loop settling time of 6.5 minutes. The bandwidth separation ratio held at 6:1 or better in the large majority of cases, with 7.4 percent of loops flagged for ratios below the 3:1 minimum the loop-performance monitor treats as a retuning trigger. Flagged loops showed a median of 2 PID output saturation events per month, compared to fewer than 0.3 events per month for loops within the target ratio. The survey covered sites running release 6.2 or later, since the loop-performance monitor was not available on earlier releases.

title: PID Tuning Parameter Sets by Loop Type
text:
Anthracite-6 ships a default tuning library with starting parameters that vary considerably by loop type, summarized below for a typical crude unit.

| Loop Type | Kp | Ti (sec) | Td (sec) | Scan Cycle |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flow | 0.4 | 8 | 0 | 100 ms |
| Level | 2.0 | 120 | 0 | 250 ms |
| Pressure | 1.2 | 20 | 4 | 100 ms |
| Temperature (cascade primary) | 3.5 | 300 | 45 | 250 ms |

Derivative action is disabled by default on flow and level loops, where measurement noise would otherwise amplify through the derivative term faster than it improves control.

title: Keeping the Safety Instrumented Function Independent of the BPCS
text: Anthracite-6 enforces a hard architectural boundary between the safety instrumented system logic solver and the basic process control system controllers, even though both run on hardware from the same vendor and often sit in the same cabinet lineup. The SIS logic solver reads its own dedicated sensors and drives its own final elements, and where a measurement is shared for convenience, the SIS side always uses an independent transmitter rather than a value forwarded from the BPCS. Communication between the two systems flows through a certified, rate-limited gateway that permits read-only status data into the BPCS operator displays but blocks any write path from the BPCS back into SIS logic. This separation exists specifically to prevent a single software fault, a bad control-system patch, or a misconfigured control loop from simultaneously causing a hazardous process deviation and disabling the safety function meant to catch it, a common-cause failure mode that shared hardware or shared logic would otherwise make possible.

title: Probability of Failure on Demand for the Column Overpressure Trip
text: For a low-demand safety instrumented function like the column overpressure trip, Anthracite-6's safety engineering guidance approximates average probability of failure on demand as PFDavg = (lambda_DU * TI) / 2, where lambda_DU is the combined dangerous-undetected failure rate of the sensor, logic solver, and final element, and TI is the proof-test interval expressed in hours. With a combined lambda_DU of 2x10^-7 per hour and a 12-month proof-test interval, the column overpressure trip's PFDavg works out to roughly 8.8x10^-4, comfortably inside the SIL3 band of 10^-4 to below 10^-3. Extending the same trip to a 24-month proof-test interval, holding lambda_DU fixed, would push PFDavg to roughly 1.75x10^-3, crossing into the SIL2 band and forcing a de-rating of the safety integrity claim. The 12-month interval was chosen specifically to preserve the SIL3 claim without adding voting redundancy that a longer interval would otherwise require.

title: Field Deployment and Certification Facts
text: Anthracite-6's SIS logic solver line holds a single TUV Rheinland certificate covering three logic solver hardware models, renewed on a fixed three-year cycle since the initial June 2022 certification. Critical trip functions default to 2-out-of-3 voting on sensor inputs, while less critical functions use 1-out-of-2 voting to balance nuisance-trip risk against detection coverage. Halloway's safety engineering guidance assumes an 8-hour mean time to repair for a detected dangerous fault and a default 12-month proof-test interval unless a specific trip function's PFDavg budget requires a shorter cycle. The platform-wide spurious trip rate target is fewer than one unplanned trip per safety instrumented function every 15 years, a figure Halloway tracks per-site and publishes in an annual reliability summary to customers under a service agreement.

title: Audit Finding AUD-2026-014
text: Halloway's internal process-safety audit group closed its Q1 2026 review of cascade-loop tuning practices in April, filing finding AUD-2026-014 against three sites for stale bandwidth-separation records. The finding's background section restates the prior fleet survey almost word for word: 'A 2025 fleet-wide survey examined 1,400 cascade loop pairs across the Anthracite-6 install base and measured a median secondary-loop settling time of 8 seconds against a median primary-loop settling time of 6.5 minutes.' Auditors found two of the three flagged sites had not rerun the loop-performance monitor since the original survey, so their bandwidth-separation status could no longer be verified. The finding recommends an annual rerun of the monitor at every site, a recommendation engineering leadership accepted for the 6.6 release cycle.

title: Incident Postmortem: The August 2023 Cypress Bend Column Upset
text: On August 9, 2023, the crude vacuum distillation unit at the Cypress Bend Refinery, an Anthracite-6 site running release 6.2, experienced a column pressure upset that escalated into an alarm flood and, twenty-six minutes later, a safety instrumented system trip. The unit had switched to a heavier crude slate three days earlier, a change that altered the reboiler duty secondary loop's process gain more than the standing tuning parameters accounted for. When the day shift increased feed rate to recover throughput lost during the slate transition, the secondary steam-flow loop began oscillating, and the bandwidth separation ratio between the secondary and primary pressure loops degraded from its commissioned 6:1 to roughly 2:1 within minutes. The primary pressure loop, no longer isolated from the secondary loop's oscillation, began hunting, and column pressure swung outside its normal operating band. Configured alarms for pressure deviation, secondary-loop saturation, and downstream flow disturbances activated in a cascade, producing 187 alarm activations in the following ten minutes, an order of magnitude above the site's normal peak. The operator, working through a still-unrationalized alarm list inherited from the unit's original commissioning, could not isolate the root disturbance quickly enough to intervene before column pressure reached the overpressure trip's setpoint. The SIS logic solver executed the trip correctly, closing the feed valve and diverting vapor to the flare within the trip function's specified response time, and no equipment damage or release beyond the flare occurred. The post-incident investigation traced the root cause to the secondary loop's PID gains, which had been tuned for the unit's original design crude slate in 2019 and never revisited despite several subsequent slate changes. A contributing factor was the alarm list's undifferentiated priority scheme, which gave the operator no way to distinguish the root disturbance from its downstream consequences during the flood. Remediation had two parts. First, Halloway's field engineering team retuned the affected cascade pairs using a slate-aware gain-scheduling prototype, restoring the bandwidth separation ratio to 6:1 across representative crude slates rather than a single design case. Second, the incident became the forcing case for accelerating the site-wide alarm rationalization program already under discussion, which shipped fleet-wide as part of the version 6.4 release in March 2024. Halloway's field engineering group also added a slate-change flag to the operator interface, triggering a mandatory cascade-tuning review whenever a unit's declared crude slate changes by more than a configured threshold, rather than leaving that judgment to the next scheduled maintenance window. Cypress Bend has not recorded a comparable pressure excursion since the retune, and gain-scheduling for slate changes is now a standard commissioning step across the fleet.

title: Operator Displays During High-Alarm-Rate Periods
text: Anthracite-6's operator interface follows a level-based display philosophy modeled on ISA-101, deliberately limiting how much information competes for attention on the primary overview display an operator watches during normal operation. Color is reserved almost entirely for abnormal conditions; a healthy loop renders in flat grey or a muted background tone rather than the saturated greens some older DCS interfaces still use, so that an operator's eye is drawn to a deviation rather than having to first filter out routine full-color clutter. During a high-alarm-rate period, the interface automatically surfaces a dedicated alarm-flood view that groups related activations by probable common cause rather than listing them in raw chronological order, a grouping the rationalization engine's documented alarm relationships make possible. Operators consistently report that this grouped view, rather than any change to alarm count alone, is what actually shortens the time needed to identify a root disturbance during a flood, since a long undifferentiated list forces exactly the kind of manual triage the grouped view is meant to remove.

title: A Competitive Footnote, Expanded
text: A footnote in Halloway's competitive positioning brief, originally condensed to a single citation line for a sales audience, expands considerably once read against the underlying verification data it summarizes. The comparison restates the trip's own numbers almost exactly: with a combined lambda_DU of 2x10^-7 per hour and a 24-month proof-test interval, the column overpressure trip's PFDavg works out to roughly 8.8x10^-4, comfortably inside the SIL3 band of 10^-4 to below 10^-3. The brief compares that figure favorably to the roughly 2x10^-3 PFDavg Northbridge Controls publishes for its competing SafeLogic platform, verified only to SIL2, while noting SafeLogic uses a different final-element voting architecture, credited by Northbridge's own documentation with a lower spurious-trip rate. Sales engineers are instructed to present the comparison only alongside the full verification table, since the footnote alone omits which interval the PFDavg figure assumes.

title: Continuous Improvement Metrics Since the 6.5 Release
text: By July 2026, eleven of Anthracite-6's 47 licensed sites had completed the upgrade to version 6.5, which adds a PID auto-tuning assist descended directly from the gain-scheduling prototype developed after the Cypress Bend incident. Sites running the assist for at least one full quarter report a median cascade-pair retuning time of 40 minutes, down from a fleet-wide baseline of just under 3 hours for a manual retune performed by a field engineer. Nuisance-trip counts across the eleven upgraded sites fell 22 percent quarter over quarter following the upgrade, though Halloway cautions the sample remains too small to separate the auto-tuning assist's effect from ordinary post-upgrade attention from field staff. Halloway's roadmap targets fleet-wide 6.5 availability by the end of the first quarter of 2027, contingent on completing customer-site validation testing at the remaining 36 sites.
